coredata.dc:title: Integrating bio-based luminescent CQDs nanosensors and factorial-design-optimized sugaring-out homogenous liquid–liquid microextraction for sensitive determination of two neurologic drugs

coredata.dc:description: The applications of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are extremely widened with time. Yet, the dependency of the current synthesis techniques on lengthy, high energy-consuming steps, and chemical substrates initiates an urgent need for a faster, less energy-consuming, and greener synthesis approach. In this study, new CQDs with nitrogen and sulfur self-doping (N,S-CQDs) were fabricated by facile one-pot microwave irradiation within 9 min. The execution of a plant waste-derived highly fluorescent nanoprobe that is synthesized from a zucchini/soy sauce mixture with an efficient microextraction platform based on QbD-optimized homogenous liquid–liquid extraction provided a rapid, high throughput, and green complemented strategy for the important neurologic drugs; entacapone (ENT) and tizanidine (TIZ). Notably, this method omits pre-derivatization, long reaction times, waste generation, and excessive volumes of organic solvents, which distinguishes it from earlier procedures. The prepared N,S-CQDs have been applied as an effective nanosensor for ENT and TIZ at λex/λem of 335/416 nm over good linear ranges of 0.20–12.0 and 0.50–20.0 µg mL−1 with r = 0.9999 and detection limits of 0.066 and 0.166 µg mL−1, respectively. The selectivity of the sensor was studied in the presence of co-exciting substances, metals, and co-administered pharmaceuticals. It was employed for analyzing both drugs in pharmaceutical tablets and testing content uniformity. Additionally, it was combined, for the first time, with sugaring-out liquid–liquid microextraction for ENT assay in human plasma with a high percentage recovery (99.95 ± 1.56%). The method capitalizes on valorization of waste biomass into valuable luminescent bio-probe, instant mix-and-read assay, and the power of factorial design to optimize the extraction conditions reliably and rapidly. The method exhibits excellent sensitivity, enabling clinical monitoring of the neurologic drugs and highlights the potential use of SUHLLME as a simple and green enrichment procedure in fluorimetric biomedical applications. Comprehensive greenness, blueness, and whiteness evaluations revealed the method’s eco-friendliness, exceptional applicability, and sustainability owing to the renewable and cheap feedstocks, recommending it to assure product quality in pharmaceutical laboratories.

head.ce:title: Integrating bio-based luminescent CQDs nanosensors and factorial-design-optimized sugaring-out homogenous liquid–liquid microextraction for sensitive determination of two neurologic drugs

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura 35516, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura 35516, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The applications of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are extremely widened with time. Yet, the dependency of the current synthesis techniques on lengthy, high energy-consuming steps, and chemical substrates initiates an urgent need for a faster, less energy-consuming, and greener synthesis approach. In this study, new CQDs with nitrogen and sulfur self-doping (N,S-CQDs) were fabricated by facile one-pot microwave irradiation within 9 min. The execution of a plant waste-derived highly fluorescent nanoprobe that is synthesized from a zucchini/soy sauce mixture with an efficient microextraction platform based on QbD-optimized homogenous liquid–liquid extraction provided a rapid, high throughput, and green complemented strategy for the important neurologic drugs; entacapone (ENT) and tizanidine (TIZ). Notably, this method omits pre-derivatization, long reaction times, waste generation, and excessive volumes of organic solvents, which distinguishes it from earlier procedures. The prepared N,S-CQDs have been applied as an effective nanosensor for ENT and TIZ at λex/λem of 335/416 nm over good linear ranges of 0.20–12.0 and 0.50–20.0 µg mL−1 with r = 0.9999 and detection limits of 0.066 and 0.166 µg mL−1, respectively. The selectivity of the sensor was studied in the presence of co-exciting substances, metals, and co-administered pharmaceuticals. It was employed for analyzing both drugs in pharmaceutical tablets and testing content uniformity. Additionally, it was combined, for the first time, with sugaring-out liquid–liquid microextraction for ENT assay in human plasma with a high percentage recovery (99.95 ± 1.56%). The method capitalizes on valorization of waste biomass into valuable luminescent bio-probe, instant mix-and-read assay, and the power of factorial design to optimize the extraction conditions reliably and rapidly. The method exhibits excellent sensitivity, enabling clinical monitoring of the neurologic drugs and highlights the potential use of SUHLLME as a simple and green enrichment procedure in fluorimetric biomedical applications. Comprehensive greenness, blueness, and whiteness evaluations revealed the method’s eco-friendliness, exceptional applicability, and sustainability owing to the renewable and cheap feedstocks, recommending it to assure product quality in pharmaceutical laboratories.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have gained popularity as fluorescence sensors owing to their remarkable chemical and physical properties, photostability, and excellent water solubility. They have been employed in vast fields such as bioimaging, sensing, drug delivery, nucleus targeting and labelling, treatment by photodynamic, photocatalysis, and numerous medical and engineering fields [1]. Since their first evolution, different processes have been proposed for CQDs synthesis, such as hydrothermal, arc laser ablation, and thermal oxidation. Yet, these procedures suffer from the need for high temperature or energy, lengthy synthesis time, and some corrosive chemicals [2]. Currently, microwave pyrolysis is considered one of the most efficient, affordable, and quick ways to create fluorescent CQDs due to its simplicity, timesaving, and accessibility [3–6]. Additionally, it offers exceptional control over the reaction parameters without emitting much heat [2]. On another side, greening the feedstock for CQDs synthesis is crucial for greening the entire synthesis process. In this context, CQDs based on natural substrates play a remarkable role due to their remarkable properties and renewable, eco-friendly, and numerous precursor sources. They have successfully stood out among diverse materials due to their outstanding properties [7]. Also, low-value natural biomass can be repurposed into valuable highly fluorescent nanomaterials. The cost-effectiveness of the natural CQDs adds to their appeal for large-scale production. In this study, new nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CQDs (N,S-CQDs) were fabricated for the first time from the combination of waste zucchini biomass and soy sauce via low-energy microwave treatment.

Zucchini (Cucurbita pepo L.) is an excellent source of vitamin C, folic acid, vitamins B6, E, and K, and carotenoids. It is a globally cultivated plant used in oil and medical fields [8]. During its production, large amounts of small, poor-appearing zucchini are discarded as waste. Thus, it is profitable to utilize waste zucchini biomass to convert valueless waste into a high-valued product to diminish pollution and costly waste disposal. Besides, soy sauce is made mainly from soybean proteins, salt, and wheat [9]. Its characteristic aroma arises from sulfur-containing compounds [9]. These facts inspired us to fabricate new N,S-CQDs from a synergistic combination of two natural carbon-rich sources with the possibility of self-doping with nitrogen and sulfur. The furnished N,S-CQDs exhibited intense blue luminescence at λex/λem 335/416 nm that was substantially quenched in the presence of two important neurologic drugs, entacapone (ENT) and tizanidine (TIZ). This behavior has been utilized for designing a fluorescent nanosensor for the two drugs. ENT (2-cyano-N,N-diethyl-3-(3,4-dihydroxy-5-nitrophenyl)propenamide) [10] has a high potency for catechol-O-methyl transferase inhibition [11]. It is the primary choice to manage Parkinson’s disease as an adjunct medication with levodopa and carbidopa. Yet, ENT overdosage would cause critical dopaminergic side effects, including vertigo, hallucinations, dyskinesia, and insomnia [12]. So, its analysis alone or concurrently with levodopa and carbidopa in pharmaceutical tablets and biological fluids is crucial. On the other hand, TIZ (5-chloro-4-(2-imidazolin-2-ylamino)-2,1,3-benzothiadiazole [10] is an alpha-2 adrenoreceptor agonist for treating spasticity in patients suffering from spinal cord injury or multiple sclerosis [13]. Because of the medication's low dose in commercial tablets (6.0 mg / tablet), the uniformity of the dosage units’ contents should be proven.

In this connection, the United States Pharmacopoeia (USP) [14] and the British Pharmacopoeia [10] state high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) methods for both drugs’ analysis in pure form. USP [14] determined them in their tablets using the same method. Certain analytical techniques have been documented in the literature for their assay. For example, ENT has been determined by HPLC [15–18], electrochemical [12,19–21], few spectrophotometry [22], and spectrofluorimetry [23,24]. Besides, TIZ was determined by HPLC [25–30], electrochemical [31–33], spectrophotometry [34,35], and spectrofluorimetry [36,37]. However, the reported methods for ENT and TIZ suffer from some shortcomings, such as low sensitivity [15,22], time consumption [15,17,20,23], and using excessive hazardous organic solvents and reagents like acetonitrile [15,16,26,27], Triton X-100 [19], tin (IV) chloride, thioacetamide [20], hydrochloric acid [23], 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane [24], and cadmium nitrate [36]. Besides, multiple sample pretreatment procedures are needed that could affect the accuracy and precision of the analytical methods. The construction of working electrodes in electrochemical procedures is complex and needs several special reagents and steps, affecting reproducibility. Additionally, HPLC and electrochemical instruments are expensive and sophisticated, requiring highly trained technicians, especially those that use mass detectors. Besides, there are two spectrofluorimetric methods reported for each of ENT and TIZ. These methods show obvious limitations where ENT assay requires reduction with harsh chemicals like Zn/HCl followed by filtration [23] and heating at high temperature for a long time with the need of plentiful chemicals [24]. On the other hand, the spectrofluorimetric assay of TIZ takes two hours for the hydrothermal synthesis of metal-based semiconductor and a further 10 min for its complete reaction with TIZ [36]. The other method [37] needs heating for a long time with NBD-Cl as a derivatizing agent.So, these procedures require costly harmful reagents, lengthy reaction times, and heating, rendering the analysis hard, expensive, and time-consuming to be used for routine analysis. Thus, there is a need for simpler spectrofluorimetric assay with enhanced performance, greener features, and higher sensitivity. One of our study’s goals is to present a facile, direct, sensitive, and user-friendly method for ENT and TIZ assay that is superior to the current fluorimetric techniques and may contribute significantly to the drugs’ analytical profile. In this vein, fluorescence nanosensors have emerged as a promising probe for analytical applications. The designed nanosensor is highly stable, sensitive, accurate, precise, and cost-efficient. The sensing process is straightforward, without multisteps or additional heating steps, achieving high throughput.

Monitoring ENT plasma level may boost its safety for treating high-risk patients. However, the reported techniques [19,23,24] are tedious and involve multistep sample pretreatments such as long-term centrifugation, filtration, and vaporization. Homogenous liquid–liquid microextraction is a growing area of liquid–liquid extraction that utilizes a small amount of water-miscible organic solvent, which decreases costs and maintains the environment and analysts’ safety [38]. In this study, sugaring-out induced homogenous liquid–liquid microextraction (SUHLLME) has been optimized via a full factorial design [39] for the extraction of ENT from plasma, followed by determination by the developed nanosensor. Sugaring-out is an innovative phase separation technique that induces the separation of acetonitrile as a distinct phase. SUHLLME is simple, fast, and economic [40]. Combination with multivariate optimization is significantly superior to traditional univariate in terms of efficiency, time, and effort [39]. The developed approach is the first fluoriemtric nanosensor to determine ENT in plasma using SUHLLME and to conduct content uniformity.

Eventually, the eco-impact and suitability of the method for real life applications have been evaluated. The greenness was assessed employing two tools: Complementary green analytical procedure index (Complex GAPI) [41] and the Analytical Greenness Calculator (AGREE) [42]. The whiteness and the most recent blueness features were evaluated reflecting the analytical performance and practicality, respectively.

As a sum, the aims of this study can be summarized as: (i) developing fast and non-expensive strategy for synthesizing natural N,S-CQDs utilizing waste zucchini and soy sauce as starting materials and microwave carbonization pathway, (ii) characterization of the new N,S-CQDs and harnessing an efficient N,S-CQDs-nanosensor for TIZ and ENT determination and quality control, (iii) optimization and implementation of green SUHLLME for ENT extraction from plasma via full factorial design. This study presents a novel, easy, affordable, rapid, green/white/blue spectrofluorimetric approach for the analysis of ENT and TIZ in complex matrix such as plasma and testing the uniformity of TIZ low-dose tablets. Green SUHLLME minimized the use of toxic organic solvents and successfully applied for ENT for the first time. This work capitalizes on the unique merits of the agro waste-based highly fluorescent nanosensor, the efficiency of the microwave-initiated synthesis process, the excellent recovery of SUHLLME, and the power of factorial design to optimize the extraction conditions reliably and rapidly. These multiple novelty sides foster the designed approach over the reported literature. The proposed approach opens the door for reusing abandoned zucchini biomass for the production of valuable CQDs. The designed method omits the need for derivatization, vaporization, or heating. In contrast to the reported methods, the designed approach offers excellent sensitivity over wide concentration ranges with unmatched simplicity, thereby broadening its applications in clinical monitoring and pharmaceutical analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental
Materials and reagents

ce:list-item.ce:para: Standards of ENT and TIZ (purity 99 % for both) were kindly supplied by AlfaCure Pharmaceuticals Company and Novartis Pharma, Egypt, respectively. Parkicapone® tablets, batch number 22267 T, each contains 200 mg of ENT and Stalevo® tablets, batch number 2222821, each contains 150, 37.5, and 200 mg of levodopa, carbidopa, and ENT, respectively. Caredalud® labelled to contain 6 mg TIZ/tablet with batch number Y2245. All tablets were purchased from the local Egyptian pharmacy. Acids, including phosphoric (86.5 %), acetic (99.85 %), and boric acids, in addition to sodium hydroxide and required sugars involving sucrose, glucose, and fructose, were acquired from El-Nasr Chemicals Co., Egypt. HPLC grade solvents like methanol and acetonitrile were brought from Fischer Scientific in Egypt. A Millipore filter membrane with a pore size of 0.45 µm (Ireland) and distilled water were utilized during the study. Soy sauces from different companies: Haday (company 1), Heinz (company 2), and Dobella (company 3).
The samples of human plasma were obtained from Mansoura University Hospital, maintained at −20 °C, and brought to room temperature before needed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Apparatus and software
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence spectra were acquired by a Cary Eclipse, Agilent Technologies Spectrofluorimeter (Lamp: Xenon flash) connected to Fluorescence managing® software (UK). The fluorimetric measurements were employed at a high sensitivity of 800 V with slit widths of 5 nm. UV–Vis spectra were determined on a Shimadzu UV 1601, Japan. Measurements of pH were done with a digital pH/mV meter (Consort NV P901). A Sonix IV (SS101H) 230 (USA), a water bath from England Cambridge Ltd., and a microwave oven of the WANSA China-based type with 800 V (WP800P28-2) were utilized throughout the experiment. Factorial design was statistically conducted by applying Minitab® Software with the release 16 (State College, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of buffer and standard solutions
ce:section.ce:para: Standard stock solutions (100 µg mL−1) of both ENT and TIZ were prepared individually in methanol and water, respectively, in 100 mL volumetric flask. Both solutions were found to be stable for one month upon refrigeration at 4 ○C.

Britton Robinson (BRB) solution (pH 2.0–12.0) included acids with a concentration of 0.04 M for each. These acids are acetic, phosphoric, and boric acids. This combination was stirred with 0.2 M NaOH to adjust the required pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Environmentally benign synthesis of biomass-derived N,S-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Imperfect zucchini, which was brought from the local market, was washed with tap then distilled water, and finally chopped into tiny segments. These segments were treated in the home mixer to get zucchini juice that was filtered through muslin cloth. Afterward, 15 mL of zucchini juice were well-mixed with 5 mL of soy sauce in a 50-mL beaker, which was heated in an ordinary household microwave for 9 min. The resulted black charred residue was sonicated for 30 min after the addition of 20 mL of distilled water, centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 20 min, and filtered with the membrane filter to get a brown solution. Ten mL of the obtained solution was transferred into 100 mL flask, and the volume was completed with distilled water. The prepared solution was wrapped in foil and was stable for ten months upon refrigeration at 4 ○C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N,S-CQDs characterization
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was taken by (JEOL JEM 2100 TEM). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained by Thermo Fischer Nicolet IS10, USA. In addition, elemental analysis was achieved via energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) (JEOL JSM 6510LV) connected to an emission electron microscope instrument, Japan. An X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Fisher Sci.), utilizing monochromatic Al Kα radiation was involved. Zetasizer (Malvern Zeta size Nano Zs90) Malvern from UK was utilized for characterization of zeta potential distribution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for ENT and TIZ determination
Calibration curves
ce:section.ce:para: For ENT, aliquots of ENT standard stock solution were pipetted into separate 10.0 mL calibrated flasks, followed by adding 1200 µL of N,S-CQDs and 1000 µL of BRB of pH 12.0. The flasks were completed to the volume with water and mixed well.

For TIZ, aliquots of TIZ solution were pipetted into another series of 10 mL volumetric flasks, followed by adding 900 µL of N,S-CQDs, and the final volume of the flasks was filled with water and blended well.

For both analytes, fluorescence intensities (F) were measured at λem 416 nm after λex at 335 nm. The fluorescence intensity of the blank (F○) was measured in the same way, excluding the analyte. Calibration curves for both analytes were constructed by graphing Ln (F○/F) versus drug concentrations (µg mL−1) and the regression equations were generated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of ENT and TIZ in their pharmaceutical tablets
ce:section.ce:para: For Parkicapone®, Stalevo®, and Caredalud® tablets: ten tablets of each were separately weighed and ground, and an appropriate amount equivalent to 10.0 mg of each drug was transferred to 100 mL volumetric flask. Eighty mL of methanol were added to the first two tablets, while 80 mL of water was used for the last one, followed by sonication for 30 min for complete extraction. After that, the final volume was reached with the same dissolving solvent, and each flask was filtered to get a solution of 100 µg mL−1. The described procedure under 2.6.1. was conducted for each tablet extract.
For each preparation, the drug content was estimated from the corresponding calibration curve or the relevant regression equation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Content uniformity testing of Caredalud® tablets
ce:section.ce:para: Ten tablets from Caredalud® tablets (6.0 mg) were further tested for their content uniformity following USP procedures [14]. The described procedure under 2.6.2. was applied to each tablet. An aliquot of TIZ stock solution of 100 µg mL−1 was transferred into 10.0 mL volumetric flask to get a final concentration of 2.0 µg mL−1, which lies in the linear range of the studied methodology. The general procedure described under 2.6.1. was adopted to that concentration. The acceptance value (AV) was calculated after the analysis of ten tablets individually.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of ENT in plasma samples using SUHLLME
ce:section.ce:para: One mL aliquots of human plasma were added to sets of falcon tubes (15 mL), then appropriate aliquots of ENT standard solution were added, and the tubes were vortexed for one min. SUHLLME was done by adding 500 µL of acetonitrile, then vortex mixing for one min. Next, 1000 mg of sucrose was added, and the tubes were vortexed again for one min, followed by centrifugation for 5 min at 6000 rpm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of ENT in plasma samples by protein precipitation (PPI)
ce:section.ce:para: We conducted a comparative study between the designed SUHLLME and classical PPI to unveil the advantages of the new approach. One mL aliquots of human plasma sample were added to falcon tubes, followed by transferring accurate volumes of ENT standard solution. The tubes were vortexed for one min. Acetonitrile as a protein precipitation agent was added to reach 5 mL in each tube, followed by vortex for one min and centrifugation for 30 min at 3600 rpm.

In both modes, the clear supernatant in each falcon tube was transferred to 10-mL volumetric flasks, followed by conducting the cited procedure under 2.6.1. Blank samples were prepared concurrently under the same conditions without ENT. The prepared concentrations of ENT were computed from the constructed calibration curve in plasma samples. All studies including biological samples were authorized by the Committee of Research Ethics, the Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Egypt and conducted in accordance with all applicable laws and faculty regulations (Approval No. 2023–28). This study did not use human volunteers or real plasma samples. The National Egyptian Blood Bank, Mansoura University Hospital, Mansoura, Egypt, supplied the “pooled plasma” sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Microwave-assisted synthesis of biomass-based N,S-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The N,S-CQDs with high quantum yield (Ф) were fabricated for the first time employing microwave radiation in a short reaction time from a novel, completely natural combination of waste zucchini juice and soy sauce. Unused zucchini biomass that is discarded as waste from agricultural fields serves as a valuable feedstock for effective fluorescent CQDs production. Zucchini is rich in vitamins (C, B6, and E), phenolic compounds, and carotenoids (lutein and zeaxanthin) [8], while soy sauce is made mainly from wheat (60 %), soybeans (56 % protein), and salt [9]. Soy sauce has some sulfur-containing compounds such as methyl furfuryl disulfide and 2-methyl-3-(methyldisulfanyl)furan. It is among the most popular condiments in East and Southeast Asian cooking and is continuing to gain significant traction in western countries [9]. The two feedstocks are renewable, environmentally benign, remarkably sustainable, and economically viable carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur sources. So, this suggests excellent self-nitrogen and sulfur doping of CQDs without any additional chemicals or surface passivation reagents.

Primarily, we studied the impact of the ratio of zucchini juice: soy sauce on the N,S-CQDs’ Ф (0:10, 10:0, 5:15, 10:10, and 15:5, v/v) while maintaining the final volume at 20 mL. Using either feedstock individually gave a lower Ф (about 4 %). It was observed that a zucchini juice and soy sauce in the ratio of 15:5 (v/v) produced the highest Ф, as outlined in Fig. 1
a. Thus, this ratio was further employed for studying radiation time. The mixture was exposed to microwave radiation from 2 to 11 min. The optimum time was 9 min, giving the highest Ф (Fig. 1 b). Further elevation of the microwave radiation time led to a decline in Ф. Bagheri et al. [43] reported the explanation of this behavior and suggested two possible reasons. The first is attributed to the formation of larger carbon particles after prolonged heating times, which reduces the fluorescence emission. The second is the potential of enhancing the degree of carbonization on prolonged microwave radiation that may result in the production of side products that reduced the fluorescence brightness.

Furthermore, the reliability and applicability of the method in different laboratories have been tested. Zucchini plants were collected at several months, including September, October, November, December, and March, and from different geographical regions in Egypt, encompassing Mansoura (Delta region), Cairo, and Assiut (Upper Egypt). Besides, soy sauces from different companies were brought and each was used as the building block for the CQDs synthesis to assure the reproducibility and repeatability of the unification process. The computed values of relative standard deviation (RSD%) were small for the sensor furnished separately across different months, from zucchini plants of diverse cultivation areas, and from soy sauces of different companies (%RSD of 2.02, 1.30, and 0.52 %, respectively). Fig. 1 c, 1d, and 1e depict the results of this study. These data ensure the reproducibility of the fabrication system and its real applicability in most laboratories over multiple domains. Fig. 2
a vividly presents the whole experimental design for the synthesis procedure of the N,S-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization analysis of the as-fabricated N,S-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The TEM image of the as-prepared N,S-CQDs (Fig. 3
a) indicates the spherical and uniformly dispersed nanomaterials with an average particle size of 4.33 ± 0.68 nm. The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 3 b) displays a broad peak at 3421 cm−1 due to N-H and O-H groups of alcohol or carboxylic acid. The band at 2950 cm−1 points to the stretching of −C-H, while the sharp band of 1650 cm−1 is indicative of C=C, C=N, or C=O. The bands at 1410, 1174, and 1124 cm−1 are indicative of the stretching vibrations of COO−, C-N, and C-O, respectively. Finally, the peaks at 848, 670, and 610 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of C-Cl, C-S/C-S-C, and S-S, respectively [44]. In addition, EDX image (Fig. 3 c) informs the percentage of elements and their surface distribution (oxygen (29.0 %), nitrogen (17.0 %), carbon (48.0 %), sulfur (1.0 %), and chlorine (2.0 %). These findings confirm the effectiveness of microwave irradiation in carbonization and elemental doping.

The XPS spectrum (Fig. 3 d) shows peaks at 285, 400, 532, 168, 199, 293, and 1071 ev, which are attributed to C1s, N1s, O1s, S2p, Cl2p, K2p, and Na1s. The C1s spectrum ( Fig. S1a ) is divided into three peaks, C-C/C=C (284.46 ev), C-N/CS/C-O/C-Cl (285.84 ev), and C=O (287.52 ev). The high resolution N1s XPS spectrum ( Fig. S1b ) depicts two peaks of pyridinic-N (398.62 ev) and C-NH2 (399.55 ev). The O1s spectrum ( Fig. S1c ) exhibits bonds C=O (530.81 ev), COH/C-O-C (532.17 ev), and O-H (535.37 ev). Also, Fig. S1d shows S2p spectrum deconvoluted into three peaks, including C-S/S-S at 163.15 ev, SO3 2− at 167.46 ev, and S=O at 168.16 ev [45]. These findings in CQDs ensure their wettability and compatibility with other materials. The zeta potential (ζ) value of CQDs is −21.1 mV (Fig. 3 e), hinting the presence of negative charge function groups like carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. This negative value signifies the colloidal stability of the fabricated N,S-CQDs due to the electrostatic repulsion force between the molecules.

Finally, UV–Vis spectroscopy and spectrofluorimetry were employed to explore the optical performance of the N,S-CQDs. The conjugate C=C bond’s π- π* transition of the carbon clusters “core state” is responsible for the peaks at 210 and 281 nm in the UV spectrum (Fig. 4
a). While the peak at 361 nm is owing to n–π* transition of the C=O and C=N function groups [46].

Also, the emission fluorescence spectra of N,S-CQDs were explored at λex from 300 to 400 nm. Fig. 4 b displays the excitation-dependent emission property of CQDs. If λex progressively shifts from 300 to 400 nm, λem exhibits red shifts. The red-shifted spectra are associated with an increase in FI over λex 300–335 nm. The FI is dropped using λex 335–400 nm. This phenomenon is attributed to the size variation of the as-synthesized CQDs and the occurrence of distinct trap states [47]. The CQDs showed the maximum fluorescence emission intensity at 416 nm when excited at 335 nm (Fig. 4 a) (quantum yield of 10.24 %).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of N,S-CQDs formation by the designed synthesis procedure
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism for CQDs formation from waste zucchini biomass and soy sauce via microwave radiation is postulated. Firstly, the feedstock directly absorbs microwave energy, resulting in direct molecular activation or confined superheating of the fragments. Then, dehydration of biomass precursors proceeds, causing decomposition of active constituents (vitamins and carotenoids in zucchini and soybean proteins, wheat, and sulfur in soy sauce). After that, somewhat condensed parts of the decomposed materials formed large nanoparticles. The subsequent decrease in nanoparticle size is caused by the ongoing intramolecular dehydration during microwave radiation [48]. Hereupon, C-C bonds and aromatic functional moieties are formed. Nucleation via the aromatic moiety's diffusion on the particles’ surface occurs after this stage. Also, the passivation of different functional groups, such as –OH, −C=O, –COOH, –NH2, etc., is involved. This surface passivation creates a protective film that stabilizes the CQDs [49]. Finally, CQDs with small-sized particles and narrow distributions are produced. The predicted formation mechanism matched the reported mechanisms [48,49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of the zucchini/soy sauce-derived nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the furnished sensor is essential to realizing its effective applicability. It was observed that the sensor has excellent fluorescence stability over a broad pH range of 2.0–10.0. This supports its significant potential in a variety of fields. The stability of the sensor at physiological pH allows its applicability in the biomedical area. Only an 11 % decrease in FI at pH greater than 10.0 (Fig. 4 c), which is caused by the formation of an intermolecular hydrogen bond leading to particles’ aggregation and fluorescence quenching [50].

Furthermore, continuous UV lamp radiation (at 365 nm) was shone on the N,S-CQDs aqueous solution for 30, 60, and 80 min to study the photostability. There was no noticeable decline in FI after 60 min, and it declined only 6 % after 80 min, demonstrating good photostability. Interestingly, after ten months of storage of the N,S-CQDs aqueous solution at 4 ◦C, no precipitation, turbidity, or significant drop in FI was observed, as it only decreased by 3 %. This may be a result of the small particle sizes and electrostatic repulsion [47]. This study demonstrated the excellent long-term stability of the prepared CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Positioning the developed CQDs-based luminescent probe among the literature
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, we investigated waste zucchini and soy sauce as a potential completely natural substitute input for the sustainable manufacturing of CQDs. Recycling agro waste biomass is an innovative breakthrough that significantly reduces conventional chemical usage. Greening both the carbon source and the synthetic process is the major merit of the developed method. The designed biomass-based CQDs are made from cheap, forthright household feedstock with potential self-doping with nitrogen and sulfur. So, the developed nanoprobe is superior to the reported ones [46,50–54] owing to its sustainability, economic efficiency, endless availability, inclusion of diverse functional groups, high Ф, stability for ten months, vibrant fluorescence, and mix-and-read assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zucchini/soy sauce-derived CQDs as nanosensor for ENT and TIZ: Principle and mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: ENT and TIZ trigger strong quenching of the fabricated sensor’s fluorescence, as displayed in Fig. S2 . There are many reasons for fluorescence quenching, like static quenching, dynamic quenching, or inner filter effect (IFE). The IFE has two types: primary and secondary, where the sample absorbs the excitation and emission wavelengths of the fluorophore, respectively [55]. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a, the UV absorption spectra of both analytes overlapped with the excitation wavelength of the N,S-CQDs. This suggests a primary inner filter effect. On top of that, the absorption spectrum of ENT overlaps with the emission wavelength of the CQDs, denoting a secondary inner filter effect.

To confirm this hypothesis, the observed fluorescence of the N,S-CQDs (Fobs) was corrected by removing IFE in the following Eq. (1) [55]:

where Fcorr refers to the corrected FI by removing IFE from Fobs (λem = 416 nm), Aex and Aem denote the absorbance at the λex and λem, respectively.

After that, the fluorescence suppressed efficiency (%E) for the observed and corrected fluorescence was determined by Eq. (2) [55], as elaborated in Fig. S3a and S3b .

We observed that after eliminating IFE, %E accounted for about 26 % and 86 % for ENT and TIZ, respectively. This indicates that IFE contributes to the quenching mechanism in both analytes. However, there are other mechanisms responsible for 74 % of ENT and 14 % of TIZ quenching effects.

To explore these mechanisms, the Stern-Volmer analysis has been conducted at three different temperatures (313, 323, and 333 K) according to Eq. (3).
(3)

K

Q u e n c h e r
where Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. In static quenching, the quencher binds to the sensor in its ground state to generate a non-fluorescent complex, while during dynamic quenching, the excited fluorophore collides with the quencher, resulting in a non-radiative loss of energy. As the temperature rises, static quenching diminishes, but dynamic quenching is directly proportional to the temperature [55]. Linear relationships between the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F◦/F) and concentrations were obtained in both analytes. Fig. S3c and S3d and Table 1 demonstrated static quenching in the case of ENT and dynamic quenching for TIZ. Hence, IFE contributes to the quenching mechanism in both drugs in combination with static quenching for ENT and dynamic quenching for TIZ. Optical characteristics of both the N,S-CQDs and ENT illustrated the static quenching mechanism. There is a variance in the spectra between N,S-CQDs alone and N,S-CQDs + ENT ( Fig. S4 ), affirming the generation of a complex between them. Fig. 2 b illustrates the potential mechanism for the generation of the ground-state complex. Charge transfer complexation is probably taking place from the amino group on CQDs’ surface to the nitro group in ENT. The amino group also interacts with oxygen in ENT via Van der Waals forces. On the other hand, hydrogen bonding occurs between carbonyl moieties of ENT in basic medium (Fig. 2 b inset) and abundant groups in CQDs (COOH, OH−, and NH2). Besides, the hydrogen bonding occurs with the nitro and amide in ENT. The strong electronegativity of the nitrogen atom of nitrile allows it to form a hydrogen bond with CQDs. Additionally, π–π stacking interaction between the aromatic core of CQDs and the benzene ring of ENT is expected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental optimization for the assay of ENT and TIZ
ce:section.ce:para:
For achieving the best analytical performance for ENT and TIZ, various experimental parameters, like pH, buffer and N,S-CQDs volumes, and reaction time, were fully optimized. BRB with pH varies from 2.0 to 12.0 and 0.1 M NaOH were tested. The outcomes highlighted that BRB of pH 12.0 provided the optimal F◦/F in the case of ENT ( Fig. S5a ). Three different buffers, BRB, carbonate, and phosphate buffers, were examined. No significant difference in F◦/F between the buffers was observed ( Fig. S5b ). BRB was utilized in the next experiment due to the highest F◦. These results showed a good correlation with the UV–Vis absorption spectra of ENT at different pH values. As presented in Fig. S6a
, the UV spectra exhibited bathochromic and hyperchromic shifts with high absorbance at both λex/λem of 335/416 nm in alkaline pH. While in an acidic medium, the absorbance was obviously lower at λem. The reason behind this behavior could be that the tautomerization rate of the nitro-containing phenolic drug is accelerated in alkaline medium (Fig. 2 b inset). The extended conjugation in the tautomeric form [54] enhances the absorbance of the drug and the formed yellow color. This enhances both primary and secondary IFE-induced FI quenching. Accordingly, different volumes of BRB of pH 12.0 were tested in the range of 500–3000 µL. It was observed that BRB volume did not have a substantial effect on F◦/F ( Fig. S6b ). Thus, 1000 µL of buffer was selected in the next experiment for ENT. In case of TIZ, none of the pH values enhanced F◦/F remarkably, as displayed in Fig. S5 . So, no need for buffer use throughout TIZ assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical approach validation
ce:section.ce:para: The optimized spectrofluorimetric methodology was validated by following ICH guidelines [56]. The plots of Ln (F◦/F) versus the concentrations of ENT or TIZ showed excellent linear correlation in the ranges of 0.20–12.0 and 0.50–20.0 µg mL−1, respectively ( Fig. S2 ). Additionally, the analytical parameters were computed as illustrated in Table S2 and the linear regression equations were:

Limits of detection (LOD) and limits of quantitation (LOQ) ( Table S2 ) were determined by applying the two equations:

Where Sa is the S.D. of the intercept and b is the slope. LOQ were 0.201 and 0.502 µg mL−1, while LOD were 0.066 and 0.166 µg mL−1 for ENT and TIZ, respectively.

Different concentrations of each of ENT and TIZ within the studied ranges were analyzed by the designed method to test its accuracy. The percentage recoveries obtained ranged from 98.45 to 101.20 % for ENT and from 98.83 to 101.48 % for TIZ, as presented in Table 2
, proving an excellent degree of accuracy. The obtained results were further compared statistically with those of the official HPLC procedures [10] by implying the F-test and t-test [57]. From Table 2, it is observed that there was no significant difference between the methodologies since the calculated outcomes are less than the theoretical ones [57].

Also, the intraday precision was evaluated for both analytes by estimating three various concentrations on the same day. Interday precision was checked by repeated estimation of the similar concentrations on three different days. Table S3 demonstrates that the current method has excellent precision.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and interference study
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity and interference studies were performed to assess the suitability of N,S-CQDs for the practical detection of ENT and TIZ. Various ions were tested, including K+, Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Cl−, and SO4 2−. Also, different excipients such as glucose, sucrose, lactose, citric acid, and sodium citrate were included in the study. Some other co-administered drugs were tested, like rasagiline, baclofen, ascorbic acid, spiramycin, aceclofenac, prednisolone, and paracetamol. As summarized in Table 3 , %FI change values were small in the range between −6.33 % and +5.25 %, since these substances had negligible influence on the N,S-CQDs’ FI. Also, selectivity against ENT structurally similar competitors was performed for levodopa and carbidopa, both separately and in admixture (Table 4 ). For this study, even though the molar concentration of the co-formulated drugs was 1, 10, and 100 times the ENT concentration, there was no significant change in FI quenching. As illustrated in Table 4, percent recoveries ranged from 97.84 to 102.91% and %RSD values were between 0.76 % and 2.12 %. These findings reflect the high selectivity for sensing both drugs in real samples without interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method applications
Assay of ENT and TIZ in pharmaceutical tablets
ce:section.ce:para: The pharmaceutical tablets were analyzed to test the real applicability of the fabricated N,S-CQDs. ENT was determined either as a single active ingredient (Parkicapone® tablets) or in combination with other medications like levodopa and carbidopa (Stalevo® tablets). Besides, TIZ was determined in Caredalud® tablets. The results of the designed methodology were statistically compared with those of the official HPLC ones [10] by t and F tests [57]. The official HPLC procedure for ENT assay uses mobile phase of tetrahydrofuran: methanol: phosphate buffer (2:44:54) with UV detection at 300 nm [10]. Regarding TIZ, gradient elution was used, involving mobile phase A, mixture of acetonitrile: sodium pentanesulfonate monohydrate (18:82) and mobile phase B, acetonitrile: sodium pentanesulfonate monohydrate (80:20) at 230 nm [10]. As observed in Table 2, there was no discernible difference between the accuracy and precision of the designed methodology and those of the official ones [10]. The findings (Table 2) demonstrated satisfactory performance, where %recoveries ranged from 98.03% to 102.11% and %RSD values were less than 1.90. Therefore, this proves the excellent applicability of the N,S-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Content uniformity testing
Assay of ENT in human plasma by SUHLLME: A comparative study
ce:section.ce:para: ENT is rapidly absorbed after oral doses with bioavailability of 29–46 %. The plasma peak concentration was reported to be 1.2–1.5 µg mL−1, occurring at approximately one hour or less after administration of a 200 mg dose in patients with Parkinson’s disease [58]. Adjunct therapy with ENT is a globally prescribed first-line approach to treat wearing-off phenomena of motor fluctuations in levodopa-treated patients. ENT administration enhances levodopa bioavailability, and it becomes more extended than taking levodopa and carbidopa alone. ENT often leads to dopaminergic events like dyskinesia, hallucinations, diarrhea, and abdominal pain, which were the most frequent adverse effects in studies of 6 to 12 months’ duration [58].Notably, it is recommended to frequently monitor ENT dose.

The study compares two extraction techniques for ENT quantification in human plasma, PPI and SUHLLME. The best percentage recoveries (≥98.0%) with lower %RSD were obtained using SUHLLME. As ENT is moderately hydrophilic with dihydroxyl, carbonyl, and amide groups, this encouraged us to apply SUHLLME (Fig. 5 ). Sugars can enhance the extraction recovery of hydrophilic analytes. Analytes are ejected from the water donor solution towards the separated water-miscible organic solvent, leading to improved mass transfer [38]. The extraction process of ENT from plasma samples necessitates a detailed examination of various factors that could affect the sensitivity of the method. This pushed the need for the use of multivariate optimization (two-level full factorial design). In SUHLLME, the examined experimental parameters were the type and amount of sugar, acetonitrile volume, and equilibration time. The starting point is to run preliminary experiments to determine the most significant factors in the extraction process.

Primarily, many sugaring-out reagents were scrutinized individually, such as glucose, fructose, and sucrose. Glucose did not cause phase separation significantly. This may be because of the low acetonitrile: sample ratio. Using larger volumes of acetonitrile to permit phase separation with glucose is avoided to save solvents, boost method greenness, and enhance ENT concentration in the extract. Thus, fructose and sucrose were selected in the factorial design. Also, the influence of sugar amount on ENT extraction was studied over the range of 800–1300 mg. Upon increasing the sugar amount to more than 1000 mg, F○/F values declined. This may be because the hydrophilic hydroxyl groups of an elevated sugar amount could form additional hydrogen bonds with other fragments, like acetonitrile, analyte, and intermolecular sugars, besides the sugaring-out force through competing with the analyte for hydrogen bonds [38]. So, a sugar amount ranging between 800 and 1000 mg was selected in the factorial design. Also, acetonitrile volume plays an essential role in achieving high extraction in SUHLLME. The effect of acetonitrile volume was studied over the range of 500–1000 µL in the factorial design.

Afterward, the impact of equilibration time after the addition of sugar to ENT was studied in the range of 0–60 min with constant experimental conditions. No significant change in F○/F value was observed for more than one hour, as shown in Fig. S7 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Full factorial design for optimization of SUHLLME
ce:section.ce:para: The suggested method exhibits high sensitivity for ENT assay in human plasma after simple pretreatment steps. It plays a worthy role in therapeutic drug monitoring, particularly for the adjustment of the dosages, thereby contributing to clinical efficacy and safety of ENT. The linear relationship was established upon plotting Ln (F○/F) versus ENT concentrations (µg mL−1) in plasma samples (Table 6 ). Percentage recovery in SUHLLME ranged from 98.00 to 102.20%, and %RSD was 1.59. The obtained outcomes prove the successful and reliable application of the method in plasma without interference from its components (Fig. 5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of traditional PPI and SUHLLME for ENT extraction from plasma
ce:section.ce:para: For the first time, ENT was analyzed in plasma samples by SUHLLME instead of classical PPI, which is applied in many published methods [19,23,24]. When the proposed nanosensor was used for ENT assay in plasma by classical PPI, the S.D., %RSD, and %error values were relatively high (Table 6). For the sake of superior analytical performance and lower LOD and LOQ values, SUHLLME was applied as a new mode for ENT extraction. SUHLLME is more sensitive with lower LOD and LOQ (0.069 and 0.208 µg mL−1) compared to (0.101 and 0.306 µg mL−1) in PPI. As presented in Table 6, the %RSD and %error values were 1.59 and 0.65% in SUHLLME. The results obtained by both modes were statistically compared [57] (Table 6). Since the calculated F value is higher than the theoretical one, it is indicated that there is a significant discrepancy between the performance of both modes regarding the F-test at a 95 % confidence level. Additionally, PPI consumes a larger volume of acetonitrile (approximately 5 mL) than SUHLLME, which needs only 500 µL. PPI needs 30 min for centrifugation, while SUHLLME requires just 5 min. This reflects the high throughput of SUHLLME for routine ENT quantification in clinical laboratories. So, it has many advantages, including low cost, minimal environmental impact, short time, high selectivity, high recoveries, and enrichment factors [40].

Green sample preparation was recently expanded into ten principles to maintain human and the environment’s safety. The AGREEprep green metric tool [59] was applied to ensure the superiority of SUHLLME over PPI regarding environmental friendliness. AGREEprep is a cutting-edge tool designed for sample preparation procedures. It was easy to interpret because each of the ten parts was assigned by colored graphical symbols [59]. As revealed in Table 6, SUHLLME possessed a higher score of 0.68, while that of PPI was 0.53, recommending the application of SUHLLME as a green and safe practice for drug analysis in complex matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessing the environmental impact of the designed method
ce:section.ce:para: The development of environmentally friendly analytical methods that minimize or eliminate the usage of hazardous materials is greatly encouraged. Also, the global trend is moving toward circular economy systems and sustainable availability of reagents. The transformation of agro-waste biomass “zero wealth” to value-added products contributes significantly to greenness and safety [60]. Thus, it became essential to assess the greenness of developed analytical methods. Since no single tool can comprehensively evaluate all relevant greenness and sustainability factors [61], different tools were utilized to generate a complete greenness profile for the developed analytical method; ComplexGAPI, AGREE, RGB12, and Blue Applicability Grade Index (BAGI). ComplexGAPI is a straightforward tool that enhances the existing GAPI metric by appending an extra hexagonal field that symbolizes the pre-analysis step, such as nanomaterial synthesis ( Fig. S10a ) [41]. It is a semi-quantitative measure that evaluates the entire pre-analysis and analysis steps. The produced pictogram ranges from green to yellow to red. However, this tool omits the validation criteria and treats few criteria as noncontinuous functions. The second tool, AGREE, is a quantitative and qualitative software that is simple, flexible, easily understandable, and covers all the principles of green analytical chemistry. It is a direct assessment tool that offers informative results including the pre-analysis steps. It generates a clock-like pictogram with 12 sections, each with a different color range with a central score [42]. However, significant sustainability dimensions are not captured like robustness, performance, and economic viability. The weight of each sector of the 12 sections is uncertain; pass over chemicals used in the pre-treatment processes [61].

White Analytical Chemistry can be considered an extension of green chemistry [62]. The White Analytical Chemistry approach provides a more in-depth review of the given method's analytical performance and practical benefits. The whiteness can be evaluated by employing a RGB12 algorithm relied on the sorting of the 12 new principles into three categories (red, green, and blue) [63]. The RGB is a quantitative and qualitative model that provides a comprehensive system of measurement to evaluate analytical methods in a global way. However, the assessment criteria are not clear, and it is a time-consuming and complex procedure [61]. On the other hand, BAGI metric supplements the existing green analytical chemistry metrics. It is a recent tool for assessing the practical aspects of an analytical procedure [64]. It is based on ten factors, producing an index score and an asteroid-like picture. However, BAGI does not offer a holistic sustainability assessment because it only addresses functional factors. Thus, it is concluded that the combination of greenness, whiteness, and blueness tools gave a complementary picture of the eco-friendliness, analytical applicability, and practicality of the developed method.

On the other hand, the designed method receives a high whiteness score of 94 ( Fig. S10c ). This is attributed to the low amount of reagents used, low waste production, simple sample preparation steps, time and cost efficiency, and good sensitivity, accuracy, and precision. Recently, some fluorimetric approaches used greenness and whiteness assessments [45,65]. The BAGI score of the developed method is 80, indicating excellent applicability and practicality, as presented in Fig. S10d . This result is attributed to the high sample throughput, minimal instrument needed, the use of accessible precursors, the omission of any derivatization or preconcentration, and the good sensitivity. Herein, the methodology relied on an energy-efficient and reproducible way to synthesize CQDs under mild conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison with different published methods for the estimation of the investigated drugs
ce:section.ce:para: The suggested method is 250–500 and even 15 Ⅹ 106 times more sensitive than the published procedures [15,16,19,22,25,26] (Table 7 ). The chromatographic procedures suffer from the need for expensive organic solvents, excessive waste generation, column preconditioning time, complex equipment, and highly trained analysts. The electrochemical methods require many chemicals, such as cetyltrimethylammoniumbromide, sodium dodecyl sulfate, and pyrrole [19,31], and some of them are toxic, stannous chloride and thioacetamide [20]. They may be restricted by the limited availability of their necessary instruments in quality control laboratories because of their high cost, complexity, lengthy preparation steps, and minimal sample throughput. Nevertheless, the spectrophotometric approach [22] is insufficiently sensitive and selective. The published spectrofluorimetric procedure [23] requires ENT reduction with Zn/concentrated HCl for 20 min. The second spectrofluorimetry [24] needs extensive sensor preparation steps like heating at 200 °C for 20 min. Reproducibility was much lower in this method [24] than the studied one (%RSD was 5.1 % compared to less than 2.02 %), and precision for the raw material was insufficient, as %RSD was 4.2 % compared to %RSD of the suggested method (less than 1.66 %).

Besides, SUHLLME used the smallest solvent volume and time of all reported methods. Whereas, the reported method [23] needed additional steps after centrifugation: filtration, vaporization at 20 °C, and reconstitution with two solvents (needed 5 mL of methanol and 0.5 mL of water).

Our method is the second fluorimetric-based probe for TIZ, with six times greater sensitivity than the first one [36]. The reported CdS sensor is prepared from CdNO3 and Na2S in sodium hydroxide solution for 2 h and modified by a corrosive chemical, thioglycolic acid [36]. The metal nanoparticles had disadvantages, including instability and aggregation of particles, synthesis challenges, and contamination during the synthesis process. Besides, cadmium ion is toxic since it interferes with DNA repair, boosts oxidative stress, and disrupts the nervous system [66]. So, it is recommended to replace metal nanoparticles with safer natural alternatives.

As a statement of significance, the suggested approach is much simpler, safer, with higher sensitivity, and a better regression line indicated by a superior r (Table 7). These features make the present approach more practical and usable in routine drug analysis than most of the reported methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: Single-step microwave synthesis of blue-emitting N,S-CQDs was established using easily available sustainable household precursors, including waste zucchini biomass and soy sauce. The furnished N,S-CQDs were characterized by spherical shape, narrow particle size range of 4.33 ± 0.68 nm, and good doping efficiency (N, 17.0 %, S, 1.0 %, and Cl, 2.0 %). The oxygen-containing functional groups on the N,S-CQDs’ surface improve water solubility and fluorescence. ENT and TIZ switch off the fluorescence of the sensor via IFE synergized by static and dynamic quenching mechanisms, respectively. The impact of various experimental parameters was carefully studied and optimized. The low LOD values of 0.066 and 0.166 µg mL−1 reveal excellent sensitivity for ENT and TIZ, respectively. The nanosensor was employed for analyzing the two drugs in pharmaceutical dosage forms as well as testing the content uniformity of TIZ commercial tablets. Additionally, it was adopted for ENT assay in human plasma with high percentage recoveries (98.0–102.2%) and without interferences employing new SUHLLME procedure. Factorial design was employed for the optimization of the extraction process as it contributed to the reduction in time, effort, and resources and the adequacy of best microextraction conditions. The developed method offers several advantages, including simplicity and greenness of the synthesis process, cost-effectiveness, rapidity, robustness and stability of the sensor, and an available instrument. The greenness, whiteness, and blueness profiles of the designed procedure reflected the exceptional level of eco-friendliness, analytical performance, and practical efficiency. So, the newly developed spectrofluorimetric methodology is an excellent candidate for successful application in quality control laboratories and pharmacokinetics studies. It opens up new avenues for solving the challenges of waste recycling, energy saving, and sustainability.
